# Scenario 17: Private Repository & Direct Patch Override

## What This Scenario Demonstrates
This scenario shows a more direct way to use a private, third-party repository. Instead of using a vendor-supplied BOM, we explicitly request the patched version of a dependency in our project's `<dependencies>` block.

This approach is simpler than the BOM method but can be more verbose if many dependencies need to be overridden. It is suitable for one-off patches or when a full BOM is not available.

## Dependency Graph
```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    subgraph Public Repos
        SpringBoot["org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter:2.5.14 (EOL)"]
    end
    subgraph Private Repo
        PatchedStarter["org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter:2.5.14.ACME"]
    end
    Root[Project Root] -- declares --> PatchedStarter

```

### Maven (pom.xml)
The `pom.xml` adds the private repository and then directly declares a dependency on the specific patched version.

```{.xml include="/demos/maven-demo/scenario-17-private-patch/pom.xml"  snippet="s17-deps"}
```


## Expected Intuition
"I know the exact patched version I need from my vendor. I will add their repository and ask for that version directly."

## Actual Resolution Results
*   **Maven**: Resolves `org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter:2.5.14.ACME`.
    *   *Reason*: The project explicitly adds the private repository, making `2.5.14.ACME` available. The direct declaration of this version in the `<dependencies>` block is the most powerful directive, so it is selected.

## Classpath Reality
The classpath contains `spring-boot-starter-2.5.14.ACME.jar`.

## Key Takeaway
A direct version declaration for a dependency is the most straightforward way to ensure a specific version is used, provided that version is available in one of the configured repositories. This method overrides all other mechanisms like BOMs or transitive versions.

## Related Scenarios
*   [Scenario 16: Private Repository & BOM Override](../scenario-16-private-bom/guide.qmd) - A more scalable approach using a vendor BOM.
